item.title: XH
item.content:encoded:
Anyone else here for XH?

Between the years 2023 and 2034, world governments approach global affairs at a radically increased level of cooperation due to the onset of a highly virulent influenza epidemic that kills approximately 1.4 billion worldwide.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
The images that were used in this article have been replaced with the following images, which are cc-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0):

Source: geograph
License: CC BY-SA 2.0
Title: Grangemouth Refinery, towards dawn
Author: Kirsty Smith
Release year: 2005

Source: Wikimedia Commons
License: Public Domain
Title: Legged Squad Support System robot prototype.jpg
Author: Tactical Technology Office, Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency, U.S. Department of Defense
Release year: 2012

item.title: XH?
item.content:encoded:
My knee-jerk reaction to XH is "oh okay they got raptured," but it really isn't the Christian Rapture, or any of the other Abrahamic religions, if all humans and animals are all "raptured." So I'm the most curious about this one.

I'm curious about XZ as well, but 001 entries are usually really dense and lead to that whole "spider web" effect where you go on a link-clicking marathon through the foundation website with no end in sight. I have to be in a certain mood to read 001 entries, so I probably won't get around to XZ for a while.

item.title: Are you sure?
item.content:encoded:
That's assuming no anomalous (while SCP-2000 can repopulate the planet and reset state of industry, it's still a loss of valuable time and resources - not to mention all the other things the Foundation has to deal with) or other events (climate change? WWIII? lack of resources?) affect viability of this operation.

Also, given all the terrible things that exist in the Foundation universe, it's no wonder things tend to result in disaster.

Let's look at the Determinative Sets:

+ show block
– hide block
XN No increase in anomalous phenomena and, while the 2349 disaster is (so far) certain, it doesn't kill off all of humanity. Post-disaster restoration via SCP-2000 possible, as well as other methods of bypassing the disaster entirely.
XB Unlike XN, restoration via SCP-2000 is likely impossible due to lack of life on Earth as well as structural damage. Not to mention this outcome has been successfully averted.
XE Similar to XN, but sooner as well as involving loss of secrecy. Hardly an improvement, not to mention it does not necessarily prevent the 2349 disaster.
XH Unknown outcome, presumed catastrophic due to inexplicable disappearance of all humans. If SCP-2000 can somehow be set for activation by timer or people sent forward with XN-SHEPHERD, this outcome may be better than XN - especially if improved social conditions are restoreable via SCP-2000 assist in preventing/circumventing the 2349 disaster.
XJ ZK-class scenario. Recovery impossible.
XO Outcome similar to XB, but possibly with more anomalies on the loose.
XR [DATA EXPUNGED], unacceptable
XS A worse version of XN, involves loss of SCP-2003. Unacceptable.
XT Probably unacceptable.
XU Irreversible XK-class scenario.
XW Dominance shift+extinction, worse than XB.
XX Also dominance shift, better than XB but worse than XN.
XZ Research ongoing. Significantly worse than XN.

XN is the best possible outcome and the reason SCP-2003 was unable to prevent the 2349 disaster so far is likely one of the following:
  • They are focused on ensuring humanity and the Foundation reach that point to begin with, and trying to affect events too soon results in sooner disasters. (See other Determinative Sets.)
  • The efforts are focused on circumventing the disaster rather than preventing it outright for the same reason.
  • The end cannot be prevented or circumvented and this is just the furthest it can be postponed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Visions of Robot?

Visions of Robodog?

Visions of the Foundation.

+1

This remember me a little the tale "Visions of Robot" where researchers send an observator to the future for know what can be wrong. The answer is… unexpected

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
that it would somehow go undetected within that time span, when we're at the CURRENT time capable of cataloging almost 20,000 such objects, most of which are far smaller than 22km across (and you'd better believe a keen eye is on the ones that are big enough to cause widespread catastrophe).

Excluding all the asteroids that surprised everyone by suddenly showing up a few weeks from buzzing Earth. Like, say, 2018 GE3 which wizzed by on the 25th of April this year, and was bug enough to kill a city. Or 2015 TB145 (discovered 21 days before its flyby), which was about 400m in diameter, and could have caused significant regional damage if it hit.

We don't have an eye on ALL asteroids. Just a lot of them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have a hard time buying into the idea that the Foundation can do both literal time travel and repopulate the entire planet, but can't figure out a way to destroy a non-anomalous asteroid that they have 300 years to prepare for. Heck, I have a hard time believing that 300 years wouldn't be enough time for the human race to figure out how to do it through non-anomalous means. Or that it would somehow go undetected within that time span, when we're at the CURRENT time capable of cataloging almost 20,000 such objects, most of which are far smaller than 22km across (and you'd better believe a keen eye is on the ones that are big enough to cause widespread catastrophe).

So, yeah, if I don't buy into XN, it's hard to buy into any of the others. Which means either SCP-2003 doesn't work period, or the Foundation is doing something to bias the results towards disaster.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 but does anyone else’s brain hurt?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That image is so fucking badass. Idk why.

item.title: Amazing
item.content:encoded: Really great concept, and the Foundation's apparent fear of the XH (H for Heaven, I assume) scenario made me laugh.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Looks like it didn't happen in this timeline :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's in three days!!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hey, that date for the GRB is only a month away.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
a literal time machine

Huh, I somehow never realized that before, but you're right. I guess execution is everything.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Kalinin managed to make a literal time machine incredibly interesting. He's a wizard, I tell you.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Throughout the 2040s and 2050s, many longstanding ethnic conflicts are resolved through the intervention of multilateral organizations, endemic poverty and hunger in areas such as sub-Saharan Africa and the Indian subcontinent is substantially decreased, and large-scale projects mitigating sea level rise are completed throughout coastal regions.
On July 29, 2059, the entire population of Earth spontaneously vanishes along with all known animal life, with no observed return within observational range.

I just hate it when that happens.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not any one SCP; just a generic blackout box meant to elicit mystery.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Which SCP is XW a reference to?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My impression is that Nuru is a random person who, due to the finicky nature of timelines, just so happens to be causally linked to certain outcomes for the planet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Maybe I'm just thick but I can't seem to wrap my head around this phrase: "Operatives must report subsequent to experiencing 98 days after SCP-2003 activation."

I think it's trying to say "Operatives must file a report detailing their experiences with SCP-2003 within 98 days". But the wording makes it sound like they need to report after they experience 98 days after 2003 (assuming they live that long).

I think a better wording would be: "Operatives must complete a report subsequent to any experiences with SCP-2003 within 98 days of SCP-2003 activation". Assuming I'm understanding it correctly. Other than that, I love the wording of this article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Timeline XO was basically the idea behind my Singularity tale seed, so maybe I should put that one on the shelf.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: well, know that the Foundation Knows, they would probably be able to launch something to divert it - nuke, gravity tug, whatever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1. Was the concept of implementing the human nervous system into BigDog something that gave you the idea for the time travelling concept as a whole SCP, or is it the other way around?

Either way, it definitely sold the article there. Disturbing although necessary, just what the Foundation is about.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It almost reminds me of some sort of thing from Chrono Trigger. Like the Epoch, except less refined, and goes to multiple timelines.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, er, this is probably one of my all-time favourite articles, partially because of what rumetzen says above and partially because the Determinative Sets are just really cool. Not sure why, but the Rapture-but-not-quite one just freaks me out.

One question, though, since I didn't see anyone else mention it here:

In particular, Nuru Diagne must be allowed to die in the manner most observations have predicted for her.

Uh, what does this mean? There's no other mention of "her" on the site, and Google just leads back here (with related searches as "ignotus" and that proctist, as a side note). So is there a reference I'm not getting, or is it meant to just be a throwaway line?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This perfectly captures what I feel the Foundation should be- a massive, hypercompetent organization that's still scrambling just to keep humanity going for a few years longer in the face of its inevitable doom.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So there's a future where the entire human population focuses all of their efforts toward breeding and caring for an incredibly venomous and hostile creature, but everyone starves to death?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The last two personnel sent to observe Determinative Set XW

So you can AIM into Determinative Sets? I thought it was random?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I strongly dislike chaos theory and predetermination taken to absurd extreme. It just feels lazy and the articles leaves impression of trying too hard to be creepy. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh my god, I love this. The alternate futures are so unique and creepy. The Gamma Ray Burst one really freaked me out. +1

item.title: Cool
item.content:encoded: I find it interesting that the entire scp can time travel, Thaumiel classes are really cool. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
XX - If it's a deliberate reference to SCP-507's trip to the spiderscape, this is great!
If not, also great. The various flavours of eternal torture are well-described and intriguing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I should not have read this in the dark at 3:30 in the morning. This is probably the most disturbing entry from the 2000 contest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is now officially one of my favorites, because ends of the world, dammit.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This actually took my breath away. I love it so much I want to hug it until it dies because I don't know the strength of my own love/arms. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Love this one. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Looking back, this is still my favorite SCP-2000 submission. I enjoyed them all, but the log for this one just stuck with me. Love it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"…the entire population of Earth spontaneously vanishes…"

Was this meant to be the entire HUMAN population, or all mammalian life forms, or even all "intelligent" life…?

Inquiring minds…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Probably "Top Secret" information clearance.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What does TS mean in "TS/2003/EYES ONLY"?

item.title: Author's Note
item.content:encoded: Self-upvoting with staff permission to counteract confirmed case of malicious downvoting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fantastic!

One question though, might you consider adding more scenarios or allowing more scenarios to be added in an experiment log? I know some people on the site think those take away from an article, and there's enough here to fully support the concept, but I often find myself getting a great deal of entertainment out of extended logs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's always been something creepy about Big Dog, no doubt, but rewatching the videos, I can't HELP but imagine there's a human brain in there.

Creepy!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The fact that they are now sending gutting people, putting their vitals into robodogs and sending them into the future makes me want to upvote this again.

You sick barstard

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
we need more of rats nest.
I don't like heros or happy endings.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I LOVE your scenarios.
This is great, but the looming threat aspect didn't end up working for me. The asteroid collision is so far out that the thing to do is work toward that and then fix it later. Even today, if we had 25 or 30 years notice, we could probably divert the rock. The Foundation's got until 2300 before they really need to worry. If anything, this SCP is reassuring that 300 years is a viable outcome!
Of course, the discussed imprecision makes all the results questionable.

Wandering Spider FTW!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: All hail Lolth! All things serve the Crimson King!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like that they're trying to avoid world peace and the Rapture/Singularity.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Re: XH - The Foundation hasn't even considered that the rapture might be better than high-stakes space-rock dodge-ball?

I'll just say that it isn't the Rapture if everyone gets to go :)

Re: Big Dog - Wouldn't they be more useful scouts if they could blend in and talk to the population? if proper humanoid androids are not feasible, at least slap an animatronic dummy on top of the rover platform. With a long coat and the right hat, the agent would at least be able to be spotted at range without immediately seeming out of place. A humanoid shell might also let them stay saner longer.

This is a very good observation. My vision of how these things operate is that they're mobile data collection platforms; they have stuff like scientific instruments on board to measure physical data, and are able to remotely monitor tv/radio transmissions and internet traffic (they're wifi enabled, of course). The complexity of the research demands a human "pilot", unfortunately for the occupant. I also imagine them operating mostly at night for field observation, working out of the safehouse referenced in the description.

They're maybe not the ideal design for what they need to do, I'll admit. But the image of BigDog skulking around at night reading your e-mails and checking the background radiation levels under your window sill was just too much to resist.

Re: General plans - Most of these sets are strictly local disasters, cosmologically speaking. Even a colony as close as Mars could easily dodge all but one. Seems to me that the Foundation should be looking into alternative preparations rather than just playing choose-your-own-continuum.

Valid point. My view of it was the Foundation found it easier to grant humanity a merciful end than it was to convince society to pursue massive offplanet colonization, but that's a fair criticism.

Thanks for the commentary!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Re: XH - The Foundation hasn't even considered that the rapture might be better than high-stakes space-rock dodge-ball?

Re: XX - Finally spider-friend gets his due!

Re: Big Dog - Wouldn't they be more useful scouts if they could blend in and talk to the population? if proper humanoid androids are not feasible, at least slap an animatronic dummy on top of the rover platform. With a long coat and the right hat, the agent would at least be able to be spotted at range without immediately seeming out of place. A humanoid shell might also let them stay saner longer.

Re: General plans - Most of these sets are strictly local disasters, cosmologically speaking. Even a colony as close as Mars could easily dodge all but one. Seems to me that the Foundation should be looking into alternative preparations rather than just playing choose-your-own-continuum.

Edit: Forgot to mention, I like it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like that the "best case" scenario is only chosen for the fact that it delays our destruction for the longest period of time.

Also, as if videos of BigDog weren't already creepy enough.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is the second best article of the contest. It has all the classic sci fi elements in a well written package. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Monomania towards the Brazilian Wandering Spider.

What.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You keep making this better and better, dammit

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: DLC Added: two bonus Determinative Sets

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

This is actually my favorite 2000 candidate that I've read so far. I especially like the direction it takes the Foundation: they're actually building stuff and experimenting and getting stuff done… instead of just passively locking up whatever they find. I like this, and upvote it in the hope that the rest of the 2000 block can take a cue here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really cool. I can't point out any one thing that makes this work, but I note a lot of effort put into careful wording, which is highly effective.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I dislike that the determinative sets are so limited, with no commentary made on that; it feels like an authorial contrivance to force the Foundation to accept XN. But considering all the excellent alternate universes established across the wiki already, this sorta… wilts in comparison.

Could be fixed if the Foundation were only currently accepting XN and new determinative sets were still being established. (Does not have to be in any sort of 'canon' with other alternate universes, to be clear.)

Otherwise, this is really excellent. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Probably Rat's Nest.
